explicit_text: I was born in Los Angeles, California, on March 18, 1933. Growing up there, it was the starting point of my life and family history. My family lived in L.A., but we often traveled north to Bakersfield for summers and holidays. Los Angeles has always been home to me and the place where many of my family stories began.

That Southern California city has always been home to me and the place where many of my family stories began.

explicit_text: California has been central to my family's story, especially places like Los Angeles and Bakersfield. My mother was born in Indiana, but we spent many summers in Bakersfield, California, traveling up Highway 99. California's hot and dry climate shaped many of my childhood memories, especially working in the fields during summer. It’s where my roots and family history are deeply planted.

explicit_text: My father is John Thomas Riddle, Sr., one of twelve children. He was about the sixth child in the family. His side of the family had a wide range of personalities, including a preacher father and siblings with very different paths, like a communist brother and a numbers racketeer. My father’s background shaped much of my understanding of family complexity.

explicit_text: My mother is Helen Louise Wheeler, born in Bloomington, Indiana, but she grew up spending summers in Bakersfield, California. She came from the Wheeler family, which included my Uncle George and my grandmother Emma, who was an amazing cook. My mother’s side of the family had a rich history and was a big part of my childhood summers and holidays.

explicit_text: Bakersfield, California, is where my mother’s side of the family lived and where we spent many summers and holidays. It’s about 115 miles north of Los Angeles, up what was then Highway 99. I have vivid memories of hot, dry weather, working in the fields, and my grandmother Emma’s incredible cooking there. Bakersfield was a place of both hard work and family togetherness.

explicit_text: World War II affected my family through my Uncle George, who served in the war. After returning, he struggled with alcoholism and became a kind of philosopher figure in the family, though he was often misunderstood. His experience in the war and its aftermath left a lasting impression on me during my childhood summers in Bakersfield.

explicit_text: George Wheeler was my uncle on my mother’s side. He was divorced and had served in World War II, but after the war, he became an alcoholic. Despite his struggles, he had a lot of wisdom and often shared his thoughts with me during summer nights under the grape arbor. He was a complex figure—both a philosopher and a family outcast.

explicit_text: Drisdom was the last name of my grandfather that I remember on my mother’s side. His presence is part of the family history I recall from my time in Bakersfield. He represents an earlier generation in my family.

explicit_text: Highway 99 was the route we took from Los Angeles to Bakersfield during summers and holidays. Back then, it was known simply as 99, and the trip was always a pleasurable journey north. Traveling this highway connected me to my mother’s family and the summers spent in Bakersfield. It’s a road tied to many family memories.

explicit_text: C. Morton Riddle was my paternal grandfather, a tall preacher with white hair who stood very straight. He was a significant figure in my father’s side of the family, which included twelve children. His presence and role as a preacher influenced the family’s character and history. I remember him as a dignified and imposing man.

explicit_text: Communism was part of my family story in an unusual way—one of my father’s brothers was a communist. This was quite strange within our family context, especially given the diversity of roles among my father’s siblings, which ranged from a preacher’s son to a numbers racketeer. It showed how varied and complex our family history was. The presence of communism in the family was a notable and surprising fact.
